title: Vision & Mission
headings: Essence of the La Montessori Way of Learning
breadcrumb: Home › Vision & Mission
body: Vision & Mission Vision "Groom each Child's Potential to Excellence" Mission The School's mission is to holistically develop individuals using Child Centric & Life-Skill based education. Also to prepare Students for Challenges of the future. Essence of the La Montessori Way of Learning Montessorians believe in allowing the child to develop according to their inner laws of development. There are four distinct planes of human growth: 1st Plane – Montessori Wing (MW) Child has a very absorbent mind. LMS works on constructivism and independence using tools of a Joyful way of Learning. 2nd Plane – Primary Wing (PW) Child is in the process of intellectual development. LMS empowers students with foundation laying and inculcates in them a Moral & Social sense. 3rd Plane – Elementary & Senior Wing (EW & SW) This is the most eventful time. LMS ensures that the students can utilise their creativity and develop holistically. This moulds a child into the adult self. 4th Plane – After Schooling Schooling at LMS leads a person to be competent, self disciplined and socially well adjusted. The Montessori Education System is characterized by an emphasis on independence, freedom within limits and respect for a child's natural psychological development as well as technological advancement in society. We believe in a constructivist model for learning rather than instructivist.

title: Principal Reports
breadcrumb: Home › Administrative Code of Conduct › Admin Reports › Principal Reports
body: OO/07/24 • Issued: 10th April 2024 Principal Reporting Format and Google Calendar Requirements Principal Reports This Office Order mandates the use of Google Calendar for daily scheduling and introduces a standardised reporting format for all school Principals across LMCS branches. Google Calendar — Mandatory Use All Principals are required to maintain an active Google Calendar for their branch. The following rules apply: Google Calendar use is mandatory for all Principals with immediate effect from the date of this Order. The next day's schedule must be uploaded to the branch Google Calendar by 5:00 PM every working day . The schedule must include: morning assembly plan, class observations, staff meetings, parent meetings, administrative tasks and any special events. The calendar must be shared with MD Academics and the Head Office Administrator. Failure to upload the schedule in advance will be noted in the Principal's performance record. Reporting Format The standardised Principal's daily report must be submitted to MD Academics by end of working day . The report follows a three-section format: Section 1 — Ongoing Tasks A bullet-point list of tasks that are currently in progress but not yet completed. Each item must include: Task description Start date Expected completion date Current status / percentage completion Any blockers or support required Section 2 — Completed Tasks A bullet-point list of tasks completed during the current day or since the last report. Each item must include: Task description Completion date Brief outcome or result Section 3 — Important Meetings A summary of all significant meetings held during the day. For each meeting, the report must include: Meeting type (staff meeting / parent meeting / administrative review / external visitor) Participants Key discussion points Decisions made or action items arising Follow-up required (if any) and responsible person Submission Method Reports are submitted via email from the Principal's official LMCS email ID to MD Academics. Subject line format: [Branch Name] Principal Report – [DD/MM/YYYY] Reports may also be entered into the designated shared Google Sheet where applicable. Compliance Non-submission of the daily report without prior intimation to MD Academics is treated as a dereliction of administrative duty. Consistent non-compliance will be escalated to the Management Director and may affect the Principal's performance evaluation.

title: COVID-19 Salary Retained Fund
breadcrumb: Home › Administrative Code of Conduct › Archived Decisions › COVID-19 Salary Retained Fund
body: OO/06/20 • Issued: 5th May 2020 COVID-19 Salary Retained Fund — Deduction and Repayment Policy Archived Decision: This Office Order was issued during the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown of 2020. The salary retained fund has since been repaid per OO/10/21. This document is maintained for historical reference only. COVID-19 Salary Retained Fund This Office Order was issued in response to the financial impact of the COVID-19 pandemic lockdown on school fee collection and operations. It outlines the decision to retain a portion of staff salaries in a managed fund and the conditions for repayment. Background The nation-wide lockdown from March 2020 resulted in school closures and a significant reduction in fee collection across all LMCS branches. To sustain school operations and protect long-term employment, the management took the decision to temporarily retain a portion of staff salaries in a COVID Fund, to be repaid once financial conditions normalised. Salary Deduction Policy A specified percentage of each staff member's monthly salary was retained in the COVID Fund for the duration of the lockdown period. The deduction percentage varied by salary band — higher-earning staff contributed a higher percentage. The retained amount was recorded for each staff member and maintained as a liability of the school. Staff received the remaining salary after deduction regularly on the scheduled salary payment date. Conditions for Repayment The retained salary would be repaid to each staff member once the school's financial situation recovered to a sustainable level. Repayment was conditional on the school resuming normal operations and fee collection returning to pre-COVID levels. The management committed to beginning repayment within a reasonable period of financial recovery, without specifying a fixed date in this Order. Non-Working Staff Rules Staff who were unable to perform any work during the lockdown (e.g., support staff with no remote work assignment) were subject to a higher deduction rate as they contributed no productive output during the closure. Staff must have completed a minimum of 12 months of physical (on-campus) work at LMCS before becoming eligible for any retained fund repayment. Staff who resigned or were terminated before the repayment period would forfeit their share of the retained fund. Communication to Staff All staff received individual communication detailing the amount retained from their salary each month. The retained amounts were included in salary slips as a line item "COVID Fund Retention". Staff were assured of the management's commitment to repayment and encouraged to continue serving during the challenging period. See also: Repayment of COVID-19 Fund (OO/10/21)

title: Repayment of COVID-19 Fund
breadcrumb: Home › Administrative Code of Conduct › Archived Decisions › Repayment of COVID-19 Fund
body: OO/10/21 • Issued: 1st April 2021 Repayment of COVID-19 Salary Retained Fund Archived Decision: This Office Order initiated the repayment of salaries retained during the COVID-19 lockdown of 2020. Repayment has been completed as per the schedules below. This document is maintained for historical reference only. Repayment of COVID-19 Fund This Office Order initiates the formal repayment of the COVID-19 Salary Retained Fund to all eligible staff members, following the financial recovery of the school. The repayment is structured in priority order to ensure the most financially vulnerable staff are repaid first. Repayment Priority Structure Priority Staff Category Rationale Priority 1 Support and non-teaching staff at the lowest salary band Greatest financial hardship during the lockdown period; dependent on salary for day-to-day expenses Priority 2 Teaching staff at junior and mid-level salary bands Moderate financial impact; significant retained amounts due to longer lockdown deduction periods Priority 3 Senior teaching staff and department heads Higher capacity to absorb the deferral; retained amounts to be repaid after Priority 1 and 2 are complete Priority 4 Administrative managers and Principals Highest salary band; repaid last once all other categories have received full repayment Instalment Plans by Staff Category Repayment is structured in instalments added to the monthly salary. The instalment period depends on the total retained amount and the staff category: Staff Category Instalment Period Method Priority 1 (Support / Non-teaching) 12 months (1 year) Monthly addition to salary; equal instalments Priority 2 (Teaching — Junior/Mid) 24 months (2 years) Monthly addition to salary; equal instalments Priority 3 (Senior Teaching / Dept. Heads) 24–36 months (2–3 years) Monthly addition to salary; instalments as per individual agreement Priority 4 (Admin Managers / Principals) 36 months (3 years) Monthly addition to salary; equal instalments commencing after Priority 1–3 repayments begin Repayment Conditions Repayment commences from April 2021 for Priority 1 staff and is staggered for subsequent priority levels. Staff who resign during the repayment period will receive any remaining unpaid balance as a lump sum in their Full & Final settlement, subject to available school funds. Staff who joined after the COVID lockdown period and therefore have no retained fund balance are not included in this scheme. The exact retained amount for each staff member was communicated individually by the Finance team along with the repayment schedule. Any discrepancy in the retained amount must be raised with the Finance team within 30 days of receiving the individual communication. Acknowledgement The management acknowledges the cooperation and patience of all staff during the COVID-19 lockdown period. This repayment scheme fulfils the commitment made in OO/06/20 to return the retained amounts once the school's financial position allowed. See also: COVID-19

title: Fee Defaulter Handling
breadcrumb: Home › Administrative Code of Conduct › School Processes › Fee Defaulter Handling
body: OO/13/24 • Issued: 17th June 2024 Fee Defaulter Identification and Follow-Up Process Fee Defaulter Handling This Office Order establishes the standard process and timelines for identifying, communicating with and managing fee-defaulting families across all LMCS branches. Roles & Responsibilities Role Primary Responsibility Fee Clerks Generate monthly defaulter lists, maintain the Parent Communication Register, send email and call reminders Admin Manager Supervise the fee follow-up process, escalate to Principal when thresholds are breached, review defaulter list accuracy Principal Conduct the monthly review meeting, authorise escalation to MD, approve fee payment plans for genuine hardship cases Account Coordinator Reconcile fee receipts with defaulter list, prepare branch-level fee collection summary for Head Office, flag unresolved defaults Monthly Defaulter Timeline Date Action Responsible 14th of month Generate fee defaulter list from school management system. List includes all students with outstanding fees above the branch threshold. Fee Clerk 15th of month Send email reminders to all defaulting parents from the official branch email ID. Begin phone call follow-up for high-default cases. Fee Clerk 25th of month Update the Parent Communication Register with call logs, responses received and any payment commitments made by parents. Fee Clerk / Admin Manager 26th of month Conduct the monthly defaulter review meeting. Principal reviews the list, Parent Communication Register and decides on escalation for unresolved cases. Principal (with Admin Manager + Fee Clerk) Branch-Wise Default Threshold Amounts The following threshold amounts determine when a family is classified as a fee defaulter and the follow-up process is initiated: Branch Threshold Amount (₹) Notes LMS 1 ₹ 8,000 Families with outstanding fees exceeding ₹8,000 are classified as defaulters LMS 2 ₹ 8,000 Families with outstanding fees exceeding ₹8,000 are classified as defaulters LMS 3 ₹ 4,000 Lower threshold due to lower fee structure; defaulter classification at ₹4,000 LMS 4 ₹ 7,000 Families with outstanding fees exceeding ₹7,000 are classified as defaulters Escalation Process Level 1 — Reminder Communication: Email and phone call by Fee Clerk (by 15th) Level 2 — Admin Manager Review: Cases not resolved after Level 1 are reviewed by Admin Manager who personally calls the parent Level 3 — Principal Intervention: Cases unresolved after Admin Manager contact are handled directly by the Principal, who may call a parent meeting Level 4 — MD Escalation: Chronic defaulters (2+ months outstanding above threshold) are escalated to MD with the full case history and the Principal's recommendation Parent Communication Register A physical Parent Communication Register must be maintained at each branch. Each entry must record: Student name and class Outstanding amount Date and mode of contact (call / email / in-person) Parent's response Payment commitment (if any) and date promised Outcome o

title: Letter & Parcel Receiving
headings: Step 5 — Fee Clerk / Admin Staff Files the Application
breadcrumb: Home › Administrative Code of Conduct › School Processes › Letter & Parcel Receiving
body: OO/10/23 • Issued: 25th September 2023 Process for Receiving Hard Copy Applications and Letters Letter & Parcel Receiving This Office Order establishes the standard 6-step process for receiving and processing all hard copy correspondence — including applications, letters and parcels — received at any LMCS branch. 6-Step Receiving Process Step 1 — Signature Required No letter, application or parcel is accepted without the delivery person or applicant obtaining an official acknowledgement. The receiving staff member (typically the front desk or administrative staff) must: Sign the delivery register or acknowledgement slip provided by the sender Note the date and time of receipt Not accept any item without proper sender identification for official correspondence Step 2 — Receival Register Entry All received correspondence is immediately logged in the school's Receival Register. The entry must include: Serial number Date and time received Sender's name and organisation Brief description of the contents (e.g., "Job Application – Science Teacher") Name of the staff member who received the item Step 3 — Principal's Signature on Register The Receival Register entry must be presented to the Principal for signature. The Principal signs the register to acknowledge that the correspondence has been officially received at the school. This step ensures accountability in the chain of custody. Step 4 — Principal's Remarks on Application The Principal reviews the correspondence and writes their initial remarks directly on the application or letter (or on an attached note if the original must be kept clean). Remarks may include: Action required (e.g., "Forward to HR", "File for reference", "Arrange interview") Priority level (urgent / normal) Person responsible for follow-up Deadline for action Step 5 — Fee Clerk / Admin Staff Files the Application After the Principal's remarks are recorded, the Fee Clerk or designated administrative staff member files the correspondence in the appropriate folder or file. Filing categories include: Job Applications Parent Correspondence Government / Department Letters Vendor / Supplier Communications Internal Communications A photocopy is made of all important correspondence before filing, and the copy is forwarded to the relevant department or person for action. Step 6 — Follow-Up and Closure The staff member responsible for follow-up (as noted in the Principal's remarks) must action the correspondence within the stipulated timeline. Once actioned: The Receival Register is updated with the action taken and closure date The original file is updated with any response sent The Principal is informed of closure for correspondence that required their initial action Important: No official correspondence may be discarded without the Principal's written authorisation. All received letters and applications must pass through this process regardless of whether they are deemed relevant.

title: Staff Meeting Guidelines
headings: Administrative Tasks & Parent/MD Meetings: 3–5 PM Window
breadcrumb: Home › Administrative Code of Conduct › School Processes › Staff Meeting Guidelines
body: OO/07/24 • Issued: 10th April 2024 Staff Meeting Timings and Scheduling Guidelines Staff Meeting Guidelines This Office Order prescribes the permitted timings for various types of staff meetings and interactions at LMCS schools to ensure that school operations, academic delivery and staff welfare are not disrupted. Permitted Meeting Timings Morning Assembly The morning assembly is used for brief announcements and notice-sharing with students and staff. Administrative messages relevant to all staff may be communicated during morning assembly. Detailed discussions or decisions must not take place during assembly time. Lunch Break Quick informal catch-ups and clarifications between the Principal and individual staff members may take place during the lunch break. However, formal staff meetings or review sessions must not be scheduled during the lunch break, as staff are entitled to a rest period. Free Periods Subject-specific discussions, one-on-one reviews and small group consultations may be scheduled during free periods. Free periods must not be used for whole-school staff meetings. Staff are expected to use free periods productively for lesson planning, corrections and preparation. Activity Teacher Consultation Before Events Before any school event, cultural programme, sports day or competition is finalised, the Principal must consult with the Activity Teacher or the staff member responsible for organising the event. This consultation must happen: At least 2 weeks before major events (Annual Day, Sports Day, Cultural Competition) At least 1 week before smaller events (classroom presentations, inter-class competitions) No event may be announced to students or parents without this prior consultation and the Principal's formal approval of the event plan. Administrative Tasks & Parent/MD Meetings: 3–5 PM Window The period from 3:00 PM to 5:00 PM is the designated slot for: Administrative and operational tasks requiring focused time (report preparation, register checking, timetable updates) Parent meetings — all parent-teacher meetings, parent grievance discussions and fee-related parent interactions MD / Management Director meetings — any meeting called by MD with the Principal or administrative staff Teaching staff should not be pulled out of teaching periods for administrative tasks during school hours. All such work is reserved for the 3–5 PM window. Combined Staff Meetings: 4–5 PM Full staff meetings or combined meetings involving all teaching and administrative staff must be scheduled between 4:00 PM and 5:00 PM . Rules for combined meetings: Notice must be given at least 24 hours in advance for regular meetings; emergency meetings may be called with 2 hours' notice via official WhatsApp group. An agenda must be shared before the meeting. Items not on the agenda should not be added without the Principal's consent at the start of the meeting. Meeting minutes must be recorded and shared with all attendees within 24 hours of the meeting. The m

title: Computer Classes
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Computer Classes
body: OO/12/24 • Issued: 6th June 2024 Computer Subject: Project-Based Evaluation Policy Computer Classes This Office Order introduces a project-based evaluation system for the Computer Science subject, replacing the traditional written examination format. The new system emphasises practical skills and applied learning. Evaluation System Computer Science is evaluated through projects and practical work rather than written examinations. The grading system uses a letter-grade scale: Grade Performance Level Description A Outstanding Exceptional project work demonstrating creativity, technical accuracy and independent thinking. Exceeds expectations in all evaluation criteria. B Proficient Good project work meeting all evaluation criteria with minor gaps. Demonstrates solid understanding of the concepts and practical skills. C Developing Satisfactory work that meets basic requirements but lacks depth or accuracy in some areas. Concepts partially understood. D Needs Improvement Work submitted does not meet minimum standards. Significant gaps in understanding or practical skills. Re-submission or remedial support required. Project Requirements Each student must complete the assigned computer project individually unless specified as a group project by the teacher. Projects are assigned at the beginning of each term and must be submitted by the specified deadline. Late submissions attract a grade reduction. Projects must be demonstrated live on the computer system; screenshots or printouts alone are not sufficient. Plagiarised or copied projects receive a D grade automatically and the student is required to resubmit an original project. Recording in TRR and CSM Computer subject grades are recorded in two official systems: TRR (Teacher Report Register): The Computer teacher records each student's project grade in the TRR under the "Computer Science" column. Grades are entered within 3 working days of project evaluation. CSM (Class Summary Matrix / School Management System): The grade is entered into the school management system or the designated digital register by the Computer teacher or the Class Teacher, as instructed by the Principal. Teacher Responsibilities The Computer teacher must assign projects aligned to the NCERT/CBSE/HPBSE curriculum for the respective class. Project rubrics must be shared with students at the time of assignment. Evaluated projects must be returned to students with written feedback within 7 working days of submission. The teacher must maintain a project record register noting the student name, class, project title, submission date and grade awarded. Parent Communication Computer project grades are communicated to parents through the school diary or the school management app at the end of each term along with other subject grades. Parents may request to view the evaluated project during the designated parent-teacher meeting.

title: EA Implementation 2025
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › EA Implementation 2025
body: OO/06/25 • Issued: 2nd May 2025 External Assessment (EA) Implementation 2025–26 EA Implementation 2025 This Office Order introduces the implementation framework for External Assessments (EA) across all LMCS branches for the academic year 2025–26. EAs replace the previous DA/OD (Day Assessment / Online Assessment) format. EA Cycles 2025–26 Five EA cycles are scheduled across the academic year: Cycle Tentative Month Subjects Covered EA 1 May 2025 English, Hindi, Maths, Science, Social Science EA 2 August 2025 English, Hindi, Maths, Science, Social Science EA 3 October 2025 English, Hindi, Maths, Science, Social Science EA 4 December 2025 English, Hindi, Maths, Science, Social Science EA 5 February 2026 English, Hindi, Maths, Science, Social Science Subjects Conducted Online The following 5 subjects are conducted in online mode for all EA cycles: English Hindi Mathematics Science / EVS Social Science / Environmental Studies Blueprints by Class Level Each class level has a specific blueprint governing marks distribution for EA. The blueprints are as follows: M2 & M3 (Montessori 2 and 3) Total Marks: 20 per subject per cycle Format: Oral + Practical activity-based; no written EA for M2/M3 Assessment recorded by Class Teacher in TRR Class I – II Total Marks: 20 per subject per EA Format: 10 Marks MCQ (Online) + 10 Marks Activity/Worksheet Duration: 30 minutes online + 20 minutes worksheet Class III – V Total Marks: 25 per subject per EA Format: 15 Marks MCQ (Online) + 10 Marks Short Answer Duration: 40 minutes online + 20 minutes written Class VI – VIII Total Marks: 30 per subject per EA Format: 20 Marks MCQ (Online) + 10 Marks Short Answer / Diagrams Duration: 45 minutes online + 30 minutes written Class IX – X Total Marks: 40 per subject per EA Format: 25 Marks MCQ (Online) + 15 Marks Short/Long Answer Duration: 60 minutes online + 30 minutes written Lab Capacity Requirement Since EAs are conducted online, the following infrastructure requirements apply: Each branch must have a functional computer lab with sufficient systems to accommodate at least one full class section simultaneously. Minimum requirement: one computer/tablet per student for the EA session. Branches that do not meet the lab capacity must report to MD Academics immediately so alternative arrangements (e.g., staggered batches) can be made. Internet connectivity must be tested at least 24 hours before each EA cycle. The Computer teacher / IT Coordinator is responsible for ensuring all systems are functional on EA day. EA Scoring & Records EA scores are automatically recorded in the school management system for online components. Written component scores must be entered by the subject teacher within 3 working days of the EA date. Cumulative EA scores replace the DA/OD columns in all report card formats from Academic Year 2025–26 onwards. The EA schedule for each cycle is announced by MD Academics at least 2 weeks in advance.

It prescribes mandatory question-paper formatting, submission routes and timelines; defines a multi-layer review, printing, storage and distribution chain; and sets clear responsibilities for teachers, IT staff, coordinators, administrators and principals. title: Science Project & Holiday Homework 2024
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Science Project & Holiday Homework 2024
body: OO/16/24 • Issued: 13th July 2024 Science Project and Holiday Homework Guidelines 2024 Science Project & Holiday Homework 2024 This Office Order provides guidelines for the Science Project assigned as part of the 2024 Summer Holiday Homework. All Science teachers and Class Teachers must ensure students complete the project as specified below. Project Categories Students are assigned one of three project categories based on their class level: Category Class Level Project Type Category A Class III – Class V Observation-based science activity / simple working model Category B Class VI – Class VIII Research Paper or Working Model (student's choice) Category C Class IX – Class X Research Paper or Working Model (student's choice) Project Themes Students may choose from any of the following 8 approved themes for their science project: Environment & Climate Change Human Body & Health Plants, Animals & Biodiversity Energy & Conservation Technology & Innovation Water & Natural Resources Space & the Universe Everyday Chemistry Research Paper Format (Categories B & C) Students choosing the Research Paper format must structure their work as follows: Title Page — Project title, student name, class, section and school name Introduction — Background and importance of the topic; research objective Materials & Methods — Data collection approach, sources used, experiments conducted Observations & Findings — Data, diagrams, photographs or charts Conclusion — Summary of findings and their significance Bibliography — List of books, websites and other references used Research papers must be handwritten in the project notebook unless the teacher has specifically approved typed submissions. Working Model Requirements (Categories B & C) Students choosing the Working Model format must: Build a functional model that demonstrates a scientific concept from the chosen theme Prepare a one-page written explanation of the scientific principle demonstrated by the model Be able to present and explain the model to the teacher and class Label all components of the model clearly Assessment Criteria Projects are assessed on 5 parameters, each carrying equal weightage (20%): Parameter Weightage What is Evaluated Relevance & Theme Alignment 20% How well the project addresses the chosen theme and scientific concepts Originality & Creativity 20% Evidence of independent thinking; not copied from textbooks or internet verbatim Scientific Accuracy 20% Correctness of scientific facts, terminology and conclusions drawn Presentation Quality 20% Neatness, organisation, labelling and visual clarity Verbal Explanation 20% Student's ability to explain the project clearly when asked Submission & Evaluation Projects must be submitted on the first day of school after the summer holidays. Working models must be brought to school on the designated Science Project Presentation Day announced by the Principal. Grades are awarded using the A/B/C/D system as per the Computer Classes evaluation policy. Gr

title: CSM Updation
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › CSM Updation
body: CSM Updation The CSM (Central School Management) portal must be fully updated before the first examination day of each term. The IT Teacher is responsible for coordinating this process. Deadline All CSM data must be updated and verified by 3:00 PM on the working day before the first examination . What Must Be Updated # Data Item Notes 1 Attendance All attendance records up to date for the term 2 Height & Weight Current measurements for all students 3 Subject Marks — CWA Class Work Assessment marks for all subjects 4 Subject Marks — RT Regular Test marks for all subjects 5 Co-Scholastic Marks Grades for co-curricular and life-skills areas 6 Student Remarks Teacher remarks for the term, reviewed and approved by the Principal Report Card Generation After the last examination of the term, report cards must be generated and ready by the last working day of the term . See Report Card Printing for the full printing and distribution process. The IT Teacher must cross-check CSM data against the physical registers maintained at each branch before the deadline. Any discrepancy must be escalated to the Principal on the same day.

title: Examination Day Process
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Examination Day Process
body: Examination Day Process Timings, protocols, and responsibilities for invigilators, teachers, and the System Coordinator on each day of the examination. Key Timings Time Activity 8:45 AM Question papers distributed to invigilators; seating verified 9:00 AM Examination begins; question papers handed to students 9:30 AM No student allowed entry after this time; late arrivals forfeit the examination Post-Examination Protocol Step Process Responsible 1 Correction: Answer sheets checked by the subject teacher of the branch where the student studies Subject Teacher 2 Counter-School Exchange: Answer sheets are exchanged between counter schools for independent verification System Coordinator 3 Super-Checking: A senior teacher reviews marks using a black pen only. Any change must be initialled HOD / Senior Teacher 4 Rectification Sheet: A Rectification Sheet is filled for every mark change identified during super-checking Super-Checking Teacher 5 Virtual Meeting: Held if content errors are found in the question paper (error in QP affects all students uniformly) System Coordinator + Principal 6 Final CSM Update: Marks entered into the CSM portal only after the Principal signs off on the Rectification Sheet IT Teacher Super-checking must be done using a black pen exclusively . Any corrections made in a different colour or without initials will not be accepted and must be redone.

title: Fine Norms
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Fine Norms
body: Fine Norms Fines are levied for violations at various stages of the examination process. All fines are deducted directly from salary. Question Paper Submission Fines Violation Fine Late submission of QP (per day, per paper) Rs. 50 / day / paper QP not submitted by the examination date Rs. 1,000 + Rs. 1,000 (i.e., Rs. 2,000 total) Formatting Fines Violation Fine Incorrect font or font size Rs. 50 per paper Incorrect margin settings Rs. 50 per paper Other formatting mistakes in question paper Rs. 5 per mistake Answer Sheet & Correction Fines Violation Fine Late return of answer sheets (per day) Rs. 50 / day Marking mistake found during super-checking Rs. 1 per mistake Fines accumulate. A teacher who submits a QP 5 days late with 3 formatting errors would incur: Rs. 250 (late) + Rs. 15 (formatting) = Rs. 265 . All fines are tracked by the System Coordinator and reported to the Admin Manager for salary processing.

title: Formatting Guidelines
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Formatting Guidelines
body: Formatting Guidelines All question papers must strictly follow the formatting specifications below. Non-compliance attracts fines as listed. Font Specifications by Subject Subject Font Size English Bookman Old Style 11 pt Hindi KrutiDev10 14 pt Sanskrit KrutiDev10 14 pt Mathematics Times New Roman 12 pt Computer Bookman Old Style 11 pt All other subjects Bookman Old Style 11 pt Margins All four margins (Top, Bottom, Left, Right) must be set to 0.63 inches for every question paper. Fine Structure for Formatting Errors Violation Fine Incorrect font or font size Rs. 50 per paper Incorrect margin settings Rs. 50 per paper Other formatting mistakes in QP Rs. 5 per mistake Formatting errors discovered after printing will still attract the full fine. Teachers are responsible for proofreading their question papers before submission to the System Coordinator.

title: Invigilation Duty
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Invigilation Duty
body: Invigilation Duty All teachers are required to perform invigilation duty during examination periods. The rules below govern minimum hours and special exemptions. Minimum Invigilation Requirement Every teacher must invigilate for a minimum of 2.5 hours per examination day . Reduced Invigilation Allowance Teachers whose own subject examinations concluded in the past 2 days may be assigned a reduced invigilation window, at the Principal's discretion, to allow sufficient time for answer sheet checking. This exception applies only when the teacher's answer-sheet checking workload directly conflicts with invigilation duty. The Principal must record this in the duty register and inform the System Coordinator. The teacher must still be available on campus for the full school day. Invigilation Responsibilities Invigilators must be in the examination hall before 8:45 AM to verify seating arrangements. No student is permitted entry after 9:30 AM ; the invigilator must enforce this strictly. Mobile phones are strictly prohibited inside the examination hall for both students and invigilators. Invigilators must maintain a strict silence environment and circulate continuously during the examination. Any instance of malpractice must be immediately reported to the Principal and documented.

title: Printing Schedule
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Printing Schedule
body: Printing Schedule The System Coordinator is responsible for coordinating the printing, distribution, and secure storage of all question papers across LMCS branches. Responsibilities The System Coordinator manages the complete printing schedule for each examination cycle. The System Coordinator must share the final printing schedule with the Admin Manager at least one week prior to the examination start date. Printed question papers must be stored in a secure room with restricted access until the day of the examination. Access to printed question papers before the examination is strictly limited to the System Coordinator and, where necessary, the Admin Manager. Any breach of question paper confidentiality will be treated as a serious misconduct matter and referred to the MD Operations. Distribution Protocol Question papers are distributed to individual branches on the morning of the examination or as per the pre-approved printing schedule. Branch Principals sign the receipt for question papers received from the System Coordinator. Any shortfall or damage to printed papers must be reported immediately to the System Coordinator. Confidentiality of question papers is non-negotiable. Any leakage — whether intentional or due to negligence — will result in disciplinary action including possible termination.

title: QP Checking Time
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › QP Checking Time
body: QP Checking Time The number of days permitted for checking answer sheets is determined by the number of students whose papers a teacher must correct. This ensures fair timelines across branches of different sizes. Checking Time Allocation Number of Students Days Allowed for Checking 1 – 35 students 1 day 36 – 70 students 2 days Important Notes The checking period begins the day after the examination for that subject. Answer sheets not returned within the permitted window will attract fines as per the Fine Norms . Teachers must complete checking, totalling, and initialling within the allocated period — super-checking by the HOD happens after this. If a teacher is absent during the checking window, the Principal must arrange an alternate checker immediately and report the situation to the System Coordinator.

title: Rectification Sheet
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Rectification Sheet
body: Rectification Sheet A Rectification Sheet must be filled for every mark change identified during super-checking. This document serves as the official record of all mark corrections and must be signed by the Principal before any update is made in the CSM portal. Standard Template Branch Subject Date of Exam Checked By Super-Checked By S.No. Roll No. Class Student Name Old Marks New Marks Total Increased Reason for Change Rows filled individually for each mark change Field Explanations Field Description Branch Name of the LMCS branch (e.g., LMS 1, LMS 3) Subject Name of the subject whose answer sheet is being rectified Date of Exam Date on which the examination was conducted Checked By Name and signature of the teacher who performed the initial marking Super-Checked By Name and signature of the HOD or senior teacher who performed super-checking Old Marks The marks as originally awarded by the checking teacher New Marks The revised marks after super-checking Total Increased Difference between new marks and old marks (positive value only; decreases are also recorded but noted separately) Reason for Change Brief explanation: e.g., "Marking error — Question 3b answer correct but marked wrong", "Totalling error", "Missed sub-question" Mandatory: No CSM portal update may be made without the Principal's signature on the Rectification Sheet. The IT Teacher must retain a copy of every signed Rectification Sheet for audit purposes.

title: Report Card Printing
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Report Card Printing
body: Report Card Printing Report cards are generated from the CSM portal and distributed to branches through a defined chain of responsibility. Printing & Distribution Chain Step Responsible Action 1 IT Teacher Generates PDF report cards from the CSM portal for all students, branch-wise. Verifies data accuracy against submitted marks and remarks. 2 System Coordinator Reviews the generated PDFs for completeness and formatting. Approves batch for printing and coordinates the physical printing process. 3 Admin Manager Receives printed report cards branch-wise. Coordinates distribution to each branch Principal for delivery to parents. 4 Branch Principal Distributes report cards to class teachers, who hand them to parents/guardians on the designated report card day. Deadline Report cards must be printed and ready for distribution by the last working day of the term . Important Rules Report cards must not be handed to students directly — they must always go to a parent or authorised guardian. If a parent has an outstanding fee dues, the report card may be withheld at the Principal's discretion pending clearance. Digital copies (PDF) may be shared via the school communication system only with written permission from the MD Operations. Any error found after printing must be corrected via a Rectification Sheet and re-printed at the earliest — the incorrect version must be retrieved and destroyed.

title: Submission & Correction Process
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Academics & Examination › Examination Process › Submission & Correction Process
body: Submission & Correction Process Question papers must be submitted well in advance and go through a structured multi-stage review chain before they are approved for printing. Submission Deadline Question papers must be submitted to the HOD at least 40 days prior to the first day of the examination. Late submissions attract fines as per the Fine Norms . Review Chain Stage Responsible Person Action 1 Subject Teacher Prepares question paper and submits to HOD 2 HOD / Senior Subject Teacher Reviews for content accuracy and blueprint compliance 3 Subject Teacher Incorporates HOD feedback and resubmits 4 IT Teacher Checks formatting (fonts, margins, layout) 5 System Coordinator Final review, approval, and printing coordination Daily Timeline During Examination The following timeline governs each examination day from correction to final mark upload: Activity Timeline Answer sheets distributed to respective teachers for checking Same day after exam Teachers complete checking (see QP Checking Time ) Within 1–2 days Super-checking by HOD / senior teacher using black pen After initial checking Rectification Sheet filled for any mark changes During super-checking Virtual meeting (if content errors found) As required Final marks updated in CSM portal after Principal approval After rectification sign-off No mark may be updated in the CSM portal without the Principal's written approval on the Rectification Sheet. Unauthorised mark changes are treated as a serious misconduct.

title: Fines
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Appointments & HR › Fines

title: Process of Hiring
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Appointments & HR › Process of Hiring
body: Process of Hiring Before Interview An Employee applies either from the Website, Facebook or in person, at the Office. The entry of such an applicant must be entered into the School Software along with the number on the Application Form. All of the Applicants must be called for Interview together. Interview The following areas must be assessed during the interview: Spouse's Occupation — Scope of transfer, monetary needs, job stability. Family Setup — Nuclear vs. joint family for support structure; children's age and school. Residence — Ease of reaching School. Academic Qualification — Type of school attended; subject background and combination; Marks in Class 10 & 12; Private vs. Regular degree; Open Schooling; gaps between years; CTET / TET / D.El.Ed / B.Ed. Professional Skills — Computer literacy: Word, Excel, PowerPoint, General. Experience — Duration, periods taken, school timing, subjects taught, salary drawn; extra hobbies; dance/music for Annual Function. Commitment to Serve — Reasons for applying; marital/family status. Minimum Monthly Salary — Ask for the lowest acceptable salary; evaluate candidates quoting high by cross-checking qualifications. Overall Attributes — Confidence, English proficiency, strengths & weaknesses; Demo class if required; student feedback. Subject to performance in the Interview, call for a Demo Class. Demo Class Observe the class for: Class Discipline Content Teaching Methodology Creativity in Teaching Half of the period must be spent observing the teacher through the CCTV cameras for Class Discipline & Changes in Attitude. Subject to satisfactory performance, the Employee could be hired. Hiring The Hiring Form needs to be filled and forwarded to the Head Office along with the Bio-Data Form within 1 week of hiring . The Salary on the Hiring Form should be filled as per the dashboard given to each Principal.

title: Resignation Rules
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Appointments & HR › Resignation Rules
body: Resignation Rules Before accepting an employee's resignation from the post held, the following must be verified: The resignation is not submitted in the middle of the academic session, as prescribed by the affiliating board. Written notice (email or application) regarding the resignation has been submitted to the Head of the Institution. The resignation must be submitted before the 10th of the month ; otherwise, it will be considered for the following month. For an employee who has served for more than 11 months at the institution, three working months' notice (90 days) or salary equivalent to the notice period must be paid. For an employee who has worked for less than 12 months at the institution, one working month's notice (30 days) or salary equivalent must be paid. If the resignation is submitted before completion of three years of service, it must be verified whether the employee has been paid salary for the vacation period and, if so, whether the salary drawn for the immediately preceding vacation period is to be refunded. A NOC (No Objection Certificate) has been obtained from the relevant departments/sections (TPR, Resource Books, Student Records, Salary Account, Student Money Accounts, and any other) of the Institution. If the above conditions are fulfilled, the Head of the Institution shall forward the resignation letter to the Governing Body with suitable opinion/remarks for orders/acceptance. The Management has the right to reject the resignation in the interest of the students. After receiving the orders/acceptance, the same shall be communicated to the employee by the Head of the Institution concerned. The salary for the month in which the resignation is submitted, and thereafter, shall not be drawn until the Management accepts the resignation. The Governing Body shall be the competent authority to accept the resignation of all employees. If an employee gives notice of resignation but withdraws it before the end of the notice period , only the date of joining changes; all privileges, perks and salaries remain the same. If an employee resigns and is off service for more than a week , they shall be treated as a fresh recruit and shall restart the probation period.

title: RRF & Security
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Appointments & HR › RRF & Security
body: Refundable Reserve Fund (RRF) & Security Refundable Reserve Fund (RRF) The RRF is a deduction from the employee's salary that the School holds as a refundable security fund . Applies to employees who joined on or after 1 January 2020 . The RRF is intended to be equal to 3 months of an employee's CTI . The RRF is deducted over 3 years at diminishing rates: Period Since DOJ RRF Deduction Rate Up to 12 months 12% 13 – 24 months 9% 25 – 36 months 6% After 36 months 0% The employee is expected to apply for a refund of the RRF after being relieved of School duties. The RRF will be refunded within 3 months of the application date. In the event of lapses in adherence to the rules, the School reserves the right to make suitable monetary deductions. However, any remaining balance shall still be payable. If any dues are pending at the time of leaving, the School will deduct these from the RRF. RRF as a Loan Employees may borrow up to 70% of the total accumulated RRF as an interest-free short-term loan. The borrowed amount must be repaid before the end of each financial year. An employee may take an employee loan a maximum of once every 2 years . The loan may be repaid in instalments deducted from the employee's monthly salary. Security Deposit The Security is an amount deducted from the salary of an employee, retained by the School in case of non-compliance with resignation rules. The balance due at the time of resignation is refundable subject to complete adherence to all resignation rules and applicable deductions. The employee may apply for refund after relieving from duties; the Security will be refunded within 3 months from the date of application. Discontinued for employees joining after 31 December 2019. Employees who joined before this date will be part of the RRF Scheme. In case of any pending dues at the time of leaving, the School shall deduct the same from the Security Fund. The balance shall still be payable.

title: Inventory & Infrastructure
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › Inventory & Infrastructure
body: Inventory & Infrastructure Use of School Infrastructure & Conveyance The employees of the School are allowed to use the Infrastructure of the School for the overall benefit of the School . The priority for the right of usage shall be exercised by the Students first. Employees are personally responsible for the maintenance and upkeep of the School Infrastructure. Employees are also allowed to use the School conveyance facility in case there are vacant seats . The first priority for the right of access to School Conveyance shall be exercised by the students. In case the School Conveyance is fully occupied by the students, the employee shall be expected to make arrangements for their own travel .

title: Code of Conduct
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Rules › Code of Conduct
body: Employee Code of Conduct Core Professional Commitment Every teacher at La Montessori Schools shall: Place the child's growth, dignity, safety, and learning at the centre of all professional decisions. Support the holistic development of each student — academic, emotional, social, moral, physical and life-skill development. Contribute to a culture of excellence, discipline, care and continuous improvement consistent with the institution's stated vision. Prepare students not only for examinations, but also for the challenges of the future through values, confidence, communication and practical competence. Conduct with Students Teachers shall: Treat every student with respect, patience, fairness and compassion, without discrimination on the basis of gender, religion, caste, language, ability, family background or academic performance. Maintain a child-friendly, safe and encouraging classroom environment that promotes curiosity, participation and confidence. Use positive discipline at all times. Humiliation, intimidation, sarcasm, abusive language, physical punishment or emotional harassment are strictly prohibited . Protect student confidentiality and handle personal, academic and behavioural information responsibly. Be attentive to student wellbeing and promptly report concerns relating to safety, abuse, neglect, bullying or mental distress through school-approved channels. Encourage student participation in co-curricular, sports, cultural, assembly and community-oriented activities. Teaching and Learning Standards Teachers shall: Plan and deliver teaching that is child-centric, inclusive, engaging and age-appropriate, in line with the school's educational philosophy. Integrate life skills, values, communication, collaboration and problem-solving into classroom practice wherever possible. Maintain high academic expectations while providing support for varied learning needs. Assess students honestly, fairly and transparently, and avoid favouritism or manipulation of marks, records or feedback. Keep classroom records, lesson plans, student work and assessment data accurate and up to date. Continuously improve subject knowledge, pedagogy, digital competence and classroom practice through reflection, training and collaboration. Professional Behaviour Teachers shall: Demonstrate punctuality, preparedness and regular attendance in all school duties including classes, assemblies, meetings, examinations, supervision, events and parent interactions. Dress and behave in a manner that reflects professionalism, modesty, self-respect and the reputation of La Montessori Schools. Use respectful language and maintain self-control in all interactions, including during conflict or stress. Refrain from gossip, defamation, harassment, bullying, intimidation or conduct that creates division among staff, students or parents. Avoid any behaviour, online or offline, that may damage the reputation, trust or educational environment of the school. Not engage in any form of c

title: Communication at LMS
headings: TPRs, Circular Registers & Other Reporting Mediums
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Rules › Communication at LMS
body: Communication at LMS Verbal / Phone Communication This form of communication should be avoided for official purposes . It is hard to keep track of and can lead to communication gaps and miscommunications. As the easiest form of communication, employees must ensure that any conversation over this mode cannot be quoted as official instruction. TPRs, Circular Registers & Other Reporting Mediums This shall be the most used mode of official communication . Each and every thing of significance shall be mentioned to the immediate superior through this medium. This should also be used to collect and store all forms of data that the employee could need at any given point of time. WhatsApp / SMS / Instant Messaging Can be used for one-sided mass communication and announcements. Must be used for coordination amongst the various employee groups. Can also be used by an employee to escalate beyond the immediate superior when necessary. Particularly useful for out-of-office hours communication. Emails / Official Letters / Applications This form of communication must be used for very important information and formal requests . All emails aimed at coordinators and MDs should be sent to the official school email address. In case of any non-academic communication or formal request, this medium must be used.

title: Staff Exit
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Staff › Staff Exit
body: Staff Exit All staff separations — whether voluntary resignation, retirement or termination — must follow the formal exit procedure. No staff member is considered to have completed service until all exit formalities are completed. Step 1 — Resignation Rules A staff member wishing to resign must submit a written resignation letter to the Principal. The resignation must adhere to the following rules: The notice period is as specified in the appointment letter (typically one month for teaching staff, two weeks for support staff). Resignation must be submitted during a working day; email resignation is only valid if acknowledged in writing by the Principal. Resignation during an examination period requires a minimum of one month's notice regardless of the appointment letter terms. Immediate resignation (without notice) results in forfeiture of the security deposit and may result in deduction of salary in lieu of notice period. The management reserves the right to accept or reject a resignation with immediate effect or to require the staff member to serve the full notice period. Step 2 — Handing Over Form Upon acceptance of the resignation, the staff member must complete the official Handing Over Form. This form documents: Transfer of all school property (keys, equipment, books, stationery) Completion of all pending reports and registers Return of official ID card, school app login credentials and email access Briefing of the incoming or replacement staff member on pending tasks Clearance from library, inventory, finance and IT departments The Handing Over Form must be countersigned by the Principal and a copy forwarded to Head Office HR. Step 3 — No Objection Certificate (NOC) Once all handing-over formalities are complete, the management issues a No Objection Certificate (NOC) to the staff member. The NOC confirms: All dues have been cleared No pending disciplinary proceedings All school property has been returned The management has no objection to the staff member joining another institution The NOC is issued within 7 working days of completion of all formalities. The NOC is not issued if there are pending dues, unreturned property or ongoing disciplinary proceedings. Step 4 — Full & Final Settlement The Full & Final (F&F) Settlement is processed by the finance department after the NOC is issued. The settlement includes: Salary for the last working month (pro-rated if applicable) Leave encashment for any unused earned leave (as per applicable rules) Refund of security deposit (if eligible as per resignation rules) Deduction of any outstanding dues, fines or advance amounts The F&F settlement amount is transferred to the staff member's registered bank account within 30 days of the last working day. A final settlement statement is provided to the staff member for their records. Note: Staff who leave without completing the notice period or handing-over formalities are not eligible for the NOC or security deposit refund, and may be reported to relevant

title: Staff Hiring
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Staff › Staff Hiring
body: Staff Hiring Once a candidate receives MD Academics' approval, the following onboarding steps must be completed before the staff member begins work. Hiring Form A formal Hiring Form is filled for every new appointment. This form captures the final agreed designation, branch assignment, salary, joining date and any special conditions. The form is signed by the appointing authority and filed in the staff record. Appointment Letter An official Appointment Letter is issued to the candidate on LMCS letterhead. The letter specifies: Designation and reporting structure Branch and grade/subject assignment Gross monthly salary and components Probation period (typically 3 months) Terms of service, notice period and conditions of employment The appointment letter must be signed and returned by the candidate before the joining date. Document Collection The following documents are collected from every new staff member at the time of joining: Original educational qualification certificates (verified and returned) TET certificate (if applicable) Previous employment experience letters Aadhar Card / Government ID proof PAN Card (for salary tax purposes) Bank account details for salary disbursement Passport-size photographs (4 copies) Medical fitness certificate (for new teaching staff) All documents are filed in the staff personal dossier maintained at the branch and a copy is forwarded to the Head Office HR file. Job Roles Document Every staff member is given the official Job Roles document for their designation. The document outlines day-to-day responsibilities, reporting lines, performance expectations and KPIs. The staff member acknowledges receipt of the Job Roles document in writing. Service Contract A Service Contract is signed between LMCS and the new employee covering the terms and duration of service, intellectual property, confidentiality and non-compete obligations. The contract is retained in the staff file. Official Email ID An official LMCS email ID is created for every new staff member. The email ID follows the format firstname.lastname@lmcs.edu.in . All official school communication must be conducted via the official email ID only — personal email accounts must not be used for school business. WhatsApp & Communication Groups New staff are added to the relevant WhatsApp groups for their branch and subject/department. Access to school communication channels is through the registered personal mobile number of the staff member. Staff must not share group content outside the group. School Mobile App Teaching and administrative staff are required to install and log in to the official LMCS school management mobile app. App credentials are issued by the IT/Admin coordinator. The app is used for attendance marking, report card access and parent communication. Social Media Guidelines Staff members must not post, share or publish any school-related content on personal social media accounts without prior written approval from the Principal or MD. title: Staff Induction
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Staff › Staff Induction
body: Staff Induction All new staff members undergo a structured induction programme before commencing independent duties. The induction is coordinated by the Principal with support from MD Academics. Job Roles Document The induction begins with a detailed walkthrough of the official Job Roles document for the staff member's designation. The document covers: Primary duties and daily responsibilities Reporting structure and line management Subject and class assignments (for teaching staff) Administrative duties (registers, reports, school events) Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) and evaluation criteria Conduct expectations and communication protocols A signed acknowledgement that the Job Roles document has been read and understood is filed in the staff dossier. Salary Mechanism Salary Components Staff are briefed on the complete salary structure during induction: Basic Salary: The fixed component as per the appointment letter. HRA / Allowances: As applicable to the designation and branch. Deductions: Including leave deductions, fines, and security deposits. Leave Deductions Staff are informed of the leave entitlement for their category and the per-day deduction rate for unauthorised or excess leave. Deductions are applied in the salary of the month in which the leave was taken or at the time of settlement. Fines Fines may be levied for: late arrival, early departure, failure to submit reports on time, violation of the dress code, or breach of conduct rules. Fine amounts are specified in the respective Office Orders and are communicated during induction. Security Deposit A refundable security deposit is deducted from the salary over the first few months of employment. The deposit amount depends on the designation. The deposit is refunded upon proper completion of notice period and handing-over formalities. It may be forfeited in cases of abrupt exit or misconduct. Recruitment Requirement Form (RRF) Staff are made aware that future internal transfers, role changes or grade changes are initiated through the RRF process and require prior approval from MD Academics. Increments Annual increments are performance-based and reviewed at the end of the academic year. The increment quantum is determined by: Annual performance evaluation score Attendance record for the year Conduct and disciplinary record MD Academics' recommendation Increment letters are issued with the new academic year appointment. There is no automatic increment; all increments are discretionary. Incentives Staff are eligible for incentives as per the schemes active at the time of their joining. Incentive categories include: Admission Incentive: For staff who generate confirmed student admissions. Performance Bonus: For achieving academic or operational targets set at the start of the year. Event Incentive: For exceptional contribution to school events or competitions. Refer to the Incentives & Bonuses page for full details of active schemes. title: Staff Operations
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Staff › Staff Operations
body: Staff Operations Day-to-day operational rules, conduct expectations, key performance indicators and performance evaluation procedures for all LMCS staff members. School Rules Code of Conduct All staff are required to uphold the LMCS Employee Code of Conduct at all times on school premises, during school events and in all official communications. This includes: Treating students, parents and colleagues with respect and professionalism Maintaining confidentiality of student records and school information Refraining from any form of corporal punishment or psychological abuse Adhering to the dress code as prescribed for the branch and designation Using only official channels for school communication For full conduct rules, refer to School Rules . Period Allotments Teaching staff are assigned a weekly period allotment as part of the timetable. Period allotments are finalised by the Principal in consultation with the Timetable Coordinator at the beginning of each term. Staff must: Be present in their assigned classroom before the bell rings for their period Not leave the classroom unsupervised during a teaching period Report any timetable conflict or scheduling issue to the Principal immediately Not swap or reassign periods without written permission from the Principal Timetabling The school timetable is prepared at the start of each academic session. Changes to the timetable during the session must be approved by the Principal. Staff involved in preparing the timetable must ensure no teacher is assigned more than the permissible maximum periods per week as per HPBSE and CBSE norms. Reporting All teaching and administrative staff are required to submit periodic reports as assigned. This includes: Daily Diary / Period Record (teaching staff) Class Teacher Weekly Report Teacher Report Register (TRR) entries Monthly academic progress and activity reports Reports must be submitted by the due date. Late submissions attract a fine as notified in the relevant Office Order. Dress Code Staff are required to wear formal attire suitable for an educational environment. Specific dress code requirements by designation and branch are as follows: Teaching Staff (Female): Formal salwar suit or saree in muted, professional colours Teaching Staff (Male): Formal shirt and trousers; tie on designated days Administrative Staff: Formal office wear as per branch guidelines PT / Sports Staff: Designated sports uniform as issued by the school Jeans, t-shirts, casual footwear and heavy jewellery are not permitted on school days. Communication Staff communication with parents must be conducted via official channels (school diary, official WhatsApp group, email). Direct personal contact between staff and parents outside school channels is discouraged and must be reported to the Principal if it occurs. Internal communication between staff and management must use the official email ID. Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Each staff category has defined KPIs that are used for performan

title: Staff Screening
headings: Step 7 — MD Academics Approval & Talking Points
breadcrumb: Home › Employee Code of Conduct › School Staff › Staff Screening
body: Staff Screening The LMCS hiring pipeline follows a structured 7-step screening process to ensure only qualified, suitable candidates are appointed to school positions. Screening Pipeline Step 1 — Application via Google Form All candidates must fill the official LMCS hiring Google Form. Walk-in applications or informal referrals are not processed until the form is submitted. The form captures personal details, qualifications, teaching experience, subject specialisation and availability. Step 2 — Initial Interview Shortlisted candidates are called for an initial interview with the school Principal or HR Coordinator. The interview assesses communication skills, subject knowledge, attitude and cultural fit with the Montessori ethos. Candidates are evaluated on a standard scoring sheet. Step 3 — Bio Data Submission Candidates who clear the initial interview submit their full Bio Data form, which includes: Personal information and contact details Educational qualifications and copies of certificates Previous employment history with reference contacts Passport-size photograph Government-issued ID proof Step 4 — Written Test A subject-specific written test is administered to assess academic competency. Teaching candidates take tests aligned to the subject and class level they will be teaching. Administrative candidates take tests on relevant administrative and communication skills. Step 5 — Teacher Interview Questionnaire Teaching candidates complete the LMCS Teacher Interview Questionnaire, which covers: Pedagogical approach and classroom management philosophy Familiarity with CBSE/HPBSE curriculum and assessment patterns Ability to handle mixed-ability classrooms Views on student discipline and parent communication Scenario-based questions on handling academic and behavioural challenges Step 6 — Interview Report The interviewing Principal or HR Coordinator prepares a formal Interview Report summarising the candidate's performance across all steps. The report includes: Scores from the written test Ratings from the Teacher Interview Questionnaire Interviewer's qualitative assessment and recommendation Proposed designation, branch and salary range The Interview Report is forwarded to MD Academics for final review. Step 7 — MD Academics Approval & Talking Points MD Academics reviews the Interview Report and Talking Points document prepared by the school. The Talking Points document outlines the candidate's strengths, any concerns, proposed salary and start date. MD Academics gives final approval before an offer is made. No appointment letter is issued without this approval. Key Requirements Teaching staff must hold a minimum of a Bachelor's degree in the relevant subject; B.Ed. is preferred. Candidates for Class 1 and above must hold TET (Teacher Eligibility Test) qualification or be actively pursuing it. Reference checks are conducted for all final candidates before the appointment letter is issued. Background verification is mandatory for staff in positi

body: OO/HES/MD(Ac.)/LK/OO/01/21 • Issued: 15th February 2021 Qualification Requirements for Teaching Staff Qualification Requirements This Office Order outlines the qualification requirements and related directives for all teaching staff employed at La Montessori Chain of Schools. All existing and newly appointed teaching staff must comply with the requirements outlined below. 1. Educational Qualifications Update All teaching staff are required to ensure their educational qualifications are current and relevant to their assigned subject and class level. Specifically: Teaching staff who do not hold a B.Ed. degree are strongly encouraged to pursue the qualification. B.Ed. is a preferred qualification for all teaching positions. Staff currently enrolled in B.Ed. or equivalent teacher training programmes must inform the Principal and provide proof of enrolment. Updated qualification certificates must be submitted to the school within 30 days of obtaining them, for inclusion in the staff personnel file and Head Office HR records. Failure to update qualification records may affect performance evaluation scores and increment decisions. 2. TET (Teacher Eligibility Test) Essentiality In accordance with the Right to Education (RTE) Act and HP government guidelines, TET qualification is mandatory for teaching staff assigned to Classes I through VIII. The following rules apply: All staff teaching Classes I–V must hold or be actively pursuing HPTET (HP State TET) or CTET (Central Board TET) Paper I. All staff teaching Classes VI–VIII must hold or be actively pursuing HPTET or CTET Paper II. Existing staff who do not hold TET must register for the upcoming TET examination. Proof of registration must be submitted to the Principal. Staff appointed to teach Classes I–VIII without TET qualification are employed on a conditional basis and must obtain TET within the time frame specified by the management. Non-compliance with TET requirements may result in reassignment to non-TET-required roles or separation from service. 3. Self-Made Question Papers All teaching staff are required to prepare their own question papers for school examinations. The following rules apply: Question papers must be prepared by the subject teacher assigned to the class, not sourced from external agencies or copied from previous years without modification. Question papers must comply with the LMCS blueprint and examination process guidelines. Refer to the Blueprints 2024 page for subject-wise marks distribution. Question papers must be submitted to the Principal for review at least 5 working days before the examination date. Failure to submit original, self-made question papers on time will be treated as a dereliction of duty and may attract a fine. 4. Use of Official Email IDs Only All official correspondence related to qualifications, TET registration, training programmes and question paper submission must be conducted using the official LMCS email ID assigned to the staff member.
